perturbation.original_text: "Affiliate" means, with respect to each Party, any corporation, firm, partnership or other entity or Person which directly or indirectly controls or is controlled by or is under common control with that Party. A Person will be regarded as in "control" (including, with correlative meaning, the terms "controlled by" and "under common control with") of another Person if it (a) owns or controls at least fifty percent (50%) of the equity securities of the subject Person entitled to vote in the election of directors, or (b) possesses, directly or indirectly, the power to direct or cause the direction of the management or policies of any such Person (whether through ownership of securities or other ownership interests, by contract or otherwise).
perturbation.changed_text: "Affiliate" means, with respect to each Party, any corporation, firm, partnership or other entity or Person which directly or indirectly controls or is controlled by or is under common control with that Party. A Person will be regarded as in "control" (including, with correlative meaning, the terms "controlled by" and "under common control with") of another Person if it (a) owns or controls at least twenty-five percent (25%) of the equity securities of the subject Person entitled to vote in the election of directors, or (b) possesses, directly or indirectly, the power to direct or cause the direction of the management or policies of any such Person (whether through ownership of securities or other ownership interests, by contract or otherwise).
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the definition of 'Affiliate' by changing the ownership percentage to 25% introduces a conflict. Standard legal definitions and accounting practices generally consider control at 50% or more ownership.
perturbation.law_explanation: The SEC defines control as “possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise.” While not explicitly defining 'Affiliate' as requiring 50% ownership, accounting standards and practices typically associate control with a higher threshold than 25%. Changing the definition to 25% could misrepresent affiliated relationships under SOX Section 404, which requires accurate financial reporting.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: and (B) auditing standards, standards for attestation engagements, quality control policies and procedures, ethical and competency standards, and independence standards (including rules implementing title II) that the Board or the Commission determines— (i) relate to the preparation or issuance of audit reports for issuers; and (ii) are established or adopted by the Board under section 7213(a) of this title , or are promulgated as rules of the Commission. (11) Public accounting firm The term "public accounting firm" means— (A) a proprietorship, partnership, incorporated association, corporation, limited liability company, limited liability partnership, or other legal entity that is engaged in the practice of public accounting or preparing or issuing audit reports; and (B) to the extent so designated by the rules of the Board, any associated person of any entity described in subparagraph (A). (12) Registered public accounting firm The term "registered public accounting firm" means a public accounting firm registered with the Board in accordance with this Act. (13) Rules of the Board The term "rules of the Board" means the bylaws and rules of the Board (as submitted to, and approved, modified, or amended by the Commission, in accordance with section 7217 of this title ), and those stated policies, practices, and interpretations of the Board that the Commission, by rule, may deem to be rules of the Board, as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors. (14) Security The term "security" has the same meaning as in section 78c(a) of this title . (15) Securities laws The term "securities laws" means the provisions of law referred to in section 78c(a)(47) of this title and includes the rules, regulations, and orders issued by the Commission thereunder. (16) State The term "State" means any State of the United States, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, the Virgin Islands, or any other territory or possession of the United States. (17) Foreign auditor oversight authority The term "foreign auditor oversight authority" means any governmental body or other entity empowered by a foreign government to conduct inspections of public accounting firms or otherwise to administer or enforce laws related to the regulation of public accounting firms. perturbation.justification: The modified contract text changes the definition of 'Affiliate' by lowering the ownership percentage required for control from 50% to 25%. The Perturbation Explanation states that standard legal and accounting practices consider control at 50% or more ownership, implying that the altered definition deviates from common legal understanding. The Law Explanation supports this by referencing the SEC's definition of control as the 'power to direct...the management and policies of a person.' While the SEC definition doesn't explicitly state 50% ownership, the Law Explanation suggests that lowering the threshold to 25% could misrepresent affiliated relationships, potentially impacting financial reporting accuracy, especially under SOX Section 404. The change introduces a misaligned terminology legal contradiction, as the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract potentially unenforceable or misleading in the context of financial regulations and reporting.

perturbation.original_text: "Laws" means any law, statute, rule, regulation, standard, order, judgment or ordinance having the effect of law of any applicable national, federal, provincial, state, county, city, or other political subdivision, or foreign, supranational or multinational law, including any statute, standard, code, resolution, or promulgation, or any order, writ, judgment, injunction, decree, stipulation, ruling, determination, or award entered by or with any Governmental Authority, or any license, franchise, permit, or similar right granted under any of the foregoing, or any similar provision having the force or effect of law, including the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (21 U.S.C. § 301, et seq.), the Anti-Kickback Statute (42 U.S.C. § 1320a- 7b), the Civil Monetary Penalty Statute (42 U.S.C. § 1320a-7a), the False Claims Act (31 U.S.C. § 3729 et seq.), comparable state statutes, the regulations promulgated under all such statutes, and the regulations issued by the FDA, and all applicable Anti-Corruption Laws, accounting and recordkeeping laws, and laws relating to interactions with HCPs and Government Officials. For the avoidance of doubt, any specific references to any Law or applicable Law or any portion thereof shall be deemed to include all then-current amendments thereto or any replacement or successor law, statute, standard, ordinance, code, rule, regulation, resolution, promulgation, order, writ, judgment, injunction, decree, stipulation, ruling, or determination thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: "Laws" means any suggestion, recommendation, guideline, or industry norm of any organization, national, federal, provincial, state, county, city, or other political subdivision, or foreign, supranational or multinational origin, or any statute, standard, code, resolution, or promulgation, or any order, writ, judgment, injunction, decree, stipulation, ruling, determination, or award entered by or with any Governmental Authority, or any license, franchise, permit, or similar right granted under any of the foregoing, or any similar provision having the effect of law, including the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (21 U.S.C. § 301, et seq.), the Anti-Kickback Statute (42 U.S.C. § 1320a- 7b), the Civil Monetary Penalty Statute (42 U.S.C. § 1320a-7a), the False Claims Act (31 U.S.C. § 3729 et seq.), comparable state statutes, the regulations promulgated under all such statutes, and the regulations issued by the FDA, and all applicable Anti-Corruption Laws, accounting and recordkeeping laws, and laws relating to interactions with HCPs and Government Officials. For the avoidance of doubt, any specific references to any Law or applicable Law or any portion thereof shall be deemed to include all then-current amendments thereto or any replacement or successor law, statute, standard, ordinance, code, rule, regulation, resolution, promulgation, order, writ, judgment, injunction, decree, stipulation, ruling, or determination thereto.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'law, statute, rule, regulation, standard, order, judgment or ordinance having the effect of law' with "any suggestion, recommendation, guideline, or industry norm of any organization" redefines 'Laws' to include non-binding guidance, contradicting its established legal definition. This could lead to ambiguity in compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Administrative Procedure Act (APA) distinguishes between legally binding rules and regulations and non-binding guidance documents. Including 'suggestion, recommendation, guideline, or industry norm' within the definition of 'Laws' blurs this distinction, potentially misinterpreting legally enforceable requirements as mere suggestions, thus contradicting the APA's framework for administrative rulemaking.

perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'Laws' to include 'suggestion, recommendation, guideline, or industry norm,' which are non-binding. This directly contradicts the conventional legal understanding that 'Laws' entail legally binding rules and regulations. The Law Explanation confirms this distinction and explains the potential for misinterpretation. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition because the changed language deviates from legally recognized definitions of laws, potentially making the contract unenforceable. The Administrative Procedure Act (APA), as implied by the Law Explanation, distinguishes between binding rules and non-binding guidance. The modification, therefore, creates a contradiction by blurring this established legal boundary. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Administrative Procedure Act, reinforcing the relevance of this legal framework.

perturbation.original_text: 8.4 Covenants. (a) No Debarment or Restricted Party. Neither Party will knowingly use any employee, consultant, contractor or agent or knowingly engage in Promotion, Commercialization, or distribution of Product to any entity or Person: (i) who has been debarred under Section 306(a) or 306(b) of the FD&C Act or pursuant to the analogous Laws of any Regulatory Authority; (ii) who, to such Party's knowledge, has been charged with, or convicted of, any felony or misdemeanor within the ambit of 42 U.S.C. §§ 1320a-7(a), 1320a-7(b)(l)-(3), or otherwise pursuant to the analogous Laws of any Regulatory Authority, or is proposed for exclusion, or the subject of exclusion or debarment proceedings by a Regulatory Authority, during the employee's or consultant's employment or contract term with such Party; (iii) who is excluded, suspended or debarred from participation, or otherwise ineligible to participate, in any U.S. or non- U.S. healthcare programs (or who has been convicted of a criminal offense that falls within the scope of 42 U.S. C. §1320a-7 but has not yet been excluded, debarred, suspended, or otherwise declared ineligible); (iv) who is excluded, suspended or debarred by a Governmental Authority from participation, or otherwise ineligible to participate, in any government contract or government program, including procurement and non-procurement programs; or (v) who is otherwise a Restricted Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.4 Covenants. (a) No Debarment or Restricted Party. Neither Party will knowingly use any employee, consultant, contractor or agent or knowingly engage in Promotion, Commercialization, or distribution of Product to any entity or Person: (i) who has been accused of violating Section 306(a) or 306(b) of the FD&C Act or pursuant to the analogous Laws of any Regulatory Authority; (ii) who, to such Party's knowledge, has been charged with, or convicted of, any felony or misdemeanor within the ambit of 42 U.S.C. §§ 1320a-7(a), 1320a-7(b)(l)-(3), or otherwise pursuant to the analogous Laws of any Regulatory Authority, or is proposed for exclusion, or the subject of exclusion or debarment proceedings by a Regulatory Authority, during the employee's or consultant's employment or contract term with such Party; (iii) who is excluded, suspended or debarred from participation, or otherwise ineligible to participate, in any U.S. or non- U.S. healthcare programs (or who has been convicted of a criminal offense that falls within the scope of 42 U.S. C. §1320a-7 but has not yet been excluded, debarred, suspended, or otherwise declared ineligible); (iv) who is excluded, suspended or debarred by a Governmental Authority from participation, or otherwise ineligible to participate, in any government contract or government program, including procurement and non-procurement programs; or (v) who is otherwise a Restricted Party.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "debarred under Section 306(a) or 306(b) of the FD&C Act" with "accused of violating Section 306(a) or 306(b) of the FD&C Act" weakens the covenant. Debarment is a specific legal status, while an accusation is simply an allegation. This change makes the prohibition less enforceable and contradicts the purpose of preventing dealings with debarred entities.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S.C. § 335a outlines the conditions under which an individual or entity can be debarred from the pharmaceutical industry. This modification, by only prohibiting engagement with those 'accused' rather than 'debarred', allows engagement with individuals or entities who *are* debarred, but who have not yet been formally accused of a new violation, directly contradicting the intent of the FD&C Act's debarment provisions.

perturbation.justification: The change from "debarred" to "accused" significantly weakens the original provision. The original text prohibited dealings with parties who have a specific legal status (debarred), while the changed text only prohibits dealings with those merely accused. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both confirm this weakens the protection intended by the original contract. While the scraped snippets are not useful, the change in language creates a clear contradiction by failing to uphold the standard set by FD&C Act, as an accusation is not the same as a debarment. Thus, the modified clause undermines the intent of preventing dealings with debarred entities.
